<!-- generated by sdlc-spec -->

# Schema Change Tracking & Reporting

## Problem Statement

When a developer edits a DataGraphs domain model with the `Schema` API
(`src/datagraphs/schema.py`) — typically after pulling the live model via
`client.get_schema()`, mutating it, and pushing it back via
`client.apply_schema()` — there is **no record of what changed**. The server
operation is a wholesale PUT (replace the entire domain model), so neither the
SDK nor the platform produces a diff or audit of the migration. Anyone who
needs to document, review, or communicate a schema migration must reconstruct
the changes by eye or by manually diffing two JSON blobs (as the throwaway
`temp/compare_schemas.py` scripts did).

The people who feel this: SDK users authoring schema migrations who want a
**human-readable changelog to attach to the migration** (PR description,
migration note, release log) describing what the migration does to the model.

Doing nothing means migrations ship without an intelligible change summary;
reviewers can't easily see intent, and there's no durable record of how a
model evolved across an editing session.

## Objective

A `Schema` instance transparently tracks every change applied to it over its
lifetime and can, on demand, emit a **net-effect changelog** of those changes
in both human-readable and structured form — accurate enough to attach to a
schema migration without hand-editing.

**Success criteria:**
- After a sequence of mutations, `schema.change_report()` returns a
  deterministic, human-readable plain-text summary of the *net* changes
  relative to the schema's state at construction.
- `schema.change_report(fmt="records")` returns the same information as a
  structured `list[dict]` for programmatic consumption.
- The report correctly collapses no-op sequences (create-then-delete; rename
  round-trips) and preserves semantic intent where a blind diff would mislead
  (renames, reorders, label-property assignment, compound subclass creation).
- No change to existing public behaviour or method signatures; tracking is
  always on and adds negligible overhead for typical schema sizes.

## Solution Approach

**Hybrid: baseline snapshot + lightweight operation log, reported as a
semantically-annotated net-effect diff.**

At construction the `Schema` captures a deep-copied **baseline snapshot** of its
internal dict. Each public mutating method appends a lightweight **operation
record** (operation name, target, salient arguments) to a change log — recorded
only on success and only at the public-call boundary.

`change_report()` computes the **net effect** as a structural/field-level
`diff(baseline, current)` — this is the source of truth for *what* changed, and
is inherently robust to no-op sequences and even to untracked mutations made
through `to_dict()`. The operation log is then used to **annotate** that diff
with semantic intent that a blind diff cannot infer: renames (so `dosage→dose`
reads as a rename, not remove+add), reorders, label-property changes, and
compound operations (so `create_subclass` reads as one entry, not a class plus
N property additions). When an entity is both renamed and modified, identity is
tracked through the rename so the two appear as **one combined record**.

Rationale: net-effect-collapsed is fundamentally a diff; folding an op log down
to net effect by replay is error-prone, whereas diffing baseline↔current is
robust by construction. The op log earns its place purely as the annotation
source for intent. The separation also means the report stays truthful about
net structure even if a caller mutates the live dict returned by `to_dict()`.

### Alternatives Considered

- **(A) Pure operation/command log** — record each mutation; report the
  chronological sequence. Rejected as the *sole* mechanism: cannot reliably
  collapse to net effect, and misses changes made outside the tracked API.
  Retained as the annotation layer.
- **(B) Pure snapshot + diff** — diff baseline↔current only. Rejected as the
  *sole* mechanism: loses semantic intent (rename vs remove+add, reorder,
  compound subclass creation). Retained as the source of truth for net change.
- **(C) Hybrid** — **chosen.** Diff for truth, op log for intent.

## Scope

### In scope
- New change-tracking state and a `change_report(fmt="text"|"records")` method
  on `Schema`, confined to `src/datagraphs/schema.py`.
- Always-on tracking, baseline captured at construction.
- Net-effect diff covering: class add/remove/modify, property
  add/remove/modify (field-level before→after), and top-level `name`/`version`
  metadata changes.
- Semantic annotation: property renames, **class** renames (via `update_class`),
  property reorders (`assign_property_orders`), label-property assignment, and
  compound operations (`create_subclass`, `apply_to_subclasses`) as single
  entries.
- Combined rename+modify records via identity tracking through renames.
- Unit tests following existing `tests/schema_test.py` conventions.

### Out of scope
- Any change to `client.py` / the HTTP layer (no auto-logging on
  `apply_schema`; the caller decides what to do with the report).
- Re-baselining / reset after construction (no `reset_history()`); to track a
  new migration, construct a fresh `Schema`.
- Markdown or JSON-string output formats (plain text + structured records only).
- Server-side or cross-instance change history.
- Persisting/serialising the change log (it is in-memory, instance-scoped, and
  explicitly excluded from `to_dict()`/`to_json()`).

## Requirements

### Must-have
- Baseline snapshot captured at construction (after any `create_from`/legacy
  conversion completes), so a loaded schema starts at **0 changes**.
- `change_report()` → deterministic plain-text net-effect changelog (default).
- `change_report(fmt="records")` → structured `list[dict]` of net changes.
- Net-effect collapsing: create-then-delete and rename round-trips produce no
  entry.
- Field-level before→after for modified properties, limited to fields that
  actually changed.
- Property renames reported as renames (not remove+add).
- Compound operations (`create_subclass`, `apply_to_subclasses`) reported as a
  single semantic entry.
- Tracking state is private and **excluded** from `to_dict()`/`to_json()` (no
  serialisation pollution).
- A mutation that raises records nothing (record on success only).
- `clone()` and `create_from()` reset the baseline to the new instance's
  current state (fresh, 0 changes).
- No breaking change to any existing public method signature or behaviour
  (including `to_dict()` continuing to return the live internal dict).

### Should-have
- Class renames (via `update_class` `new_name`) reported as renames.
- Property reorders (`assign_property_orders`) reported as a reorder.
- Label-property assignment (`assign_label_property`) annotated semantically.
- Combined rename+modify on one entity rendered as a single record.
- Deterministic ordering of report entries (by class, then a fixed operation
  order) so reports are stable/diffable in version control.

### Nice-to-have
- A summary count/header line in the plain-text report (e.g. "Schema changes (3):").
- Inherited-property count annotation on `create_subclass` entries
  (e.g. "+6 inherited properties").

## Constraints
- Python ≥ 3.12; stdlib only for this module (no new dependencies — matches
  `schema.py` today, which uses only `json`/`datetime`/`typing`).
- Must preserve the existing typed (`py.typed`), Sphinx-docstring,
  `SchemaError`-rooted-exception conventions.
- Must not alter the wire format produced by `to_dict()`/`to_json()`.

## Affected Systems
- **`src/datagraphs/schema.py`** — the only production file changed. Adds
  tracking state to `__init__`, instrumentation across the ~14 public mutating
  methods (`create_class`, `create_subclass`, `update_class`, `delete_class`,
  `assign_label_property`, `assign_label_autogen`, `assign_baseclass`,
  `assign_class_description`, `create_property`, `update_property`,
  `rename_property`, `delete_property`, `assign_property_orders`,
  `update_schema_metadata`), and the new diff/report logic plus
  `change_report()`.
- **`tests/schema_test.py`** — new test classes for tracking & reporting.
- **`CHANGELOG.md`** — new "Added" entry for the release.
- Not changed: `client.py`, `dataset.py`, `gateway.py`, `enums.py`,
  `utils.py`, `__init__.py` (no new top-level export required unless desired).

## Technical Context
- `Schema` wraps a single `self._schema` dict
  (`{name, createdDate, lastModifiedDate, classes:[...]}`); classes hold
  `properties` lists. All mutations operate on this dict in memory.
- Several public methods are **compound**: `create_subclass` calls
  `create_class` + `create_property` repeatedly; `apply_to_subclasses=True`
  recurses `create_property`/`update_property` across subclasses. Instrumentation
  must record at the **public-call boundary only** (a re-entrancy/depth guard),
  or compound operations double-log.
- `update_property` mutates only the fields whose arguments are non-`None`, so
  a faithful field-level diff (baseline↔current) — not an argument capture —
  is required to report exactly what changed.
- `clone()` already round-trips via `create_from(json.loads(json.dumps(...)))`,
  giving a natural deep-copy idiom to reuse for baseline capture.
- `to_dict()` returns the **live** internal dict (aliased, not copied), so
  callers can mutate state outside the tracked API; the baseline↔current diff
  handles this correctly (raw structural diff, no semantic label).
- `createdDate`/`lastModifiedDate` are rewritten on most mutations and must be
  **excluded** from the diff or every report would show spurious date changes.

## Non-Functional Requirements
- **Performance/memory:** one deep copy of the schema dict at construction plus
  a small per-operation record. Typical domain models are modest (tens of
  classes); overhead is negligible. The diff runs only when `change_report()`
  is called, not on every mutation. No O(n²) traversals — diff classes and
  properties via name-keyed maps (O(n)).
- **Determinism:** identical mutation sequences must produce byte-identical
  reports (stable ordering, no reliance on dict insertion quirks for output
  order beyond a defined sort).
- **Encapsulation:** tracking attributes are private and never serialised.
- **Typing/docs:** fully typed, Sphinx-style docstrings consistent with the
  module.

## Key Decisions
1. **Hybrid (diff for truth, op log for intent)** over pure log or pure diff.
2. **Baseline = state at construction**; `clone`/`create_from` reset it.
3. **Net-effect collapsed** report (not full chronological audit).
4. **Single `change_report(fmt=...)`** entry point; `fmt="text"` (default) →
   `str`, `fmt="records"` → `list[dict]`.
5. **Always-on** tracking (no opt-in flag).
6. **Semantic annotation where it matters**: renames (class + property),
   reorders, label-property assignment, compound subclass creation.
7. **Compound ops = single semantic entry** via public-boundary recording guard.
8. **No re-baseline** method; construction-scoped lifetime.
9. **Rename+modify = one combined record** via identity tracking through renames.
10. **Untracked `to_dict()` edits show as raw structural diff** (no special
    handling; not ignored, `to_dict()` not changed to copy).
11. **Scope confined to `schema.py`**; no client/HTTP changes.
12. **Top-level `name`/`version` changes reported; date fields excluded.**

## Scenario Walkthroughs

**Happy path — edit a loaded model and document the migration:**
```python
schema = client.get_schema()                 # baseline = loaded state, 0 changes
schema.create_class("Drug", parent_class_name="Substance")
schema.rename_property("Substance", "dosage", "dose")
schema.update_property("Substance", "dose", is_optional=False)
schema.delete_property("Substance", "deprecatedCode")
print(schema.change_report())                # plain-text net-effect changelog
client.apply_schema(schema)                  # push migration
```
Expected report (illustrative): `Drug [new class]`; on `Substance`, a single
combined entry `dosage → dose [renamed]; isOptional: true → false [modified]`;
`deprecatedCode [removed]`. Intermediate `lastModifiedDate` churn suppressed.

**No-op collapse:** `create_class("Temp")` then `delete_class("Temp")` →
report contains **no** entry for `Temp`. Likewise `rename_property` A→B then
B→A → no entry.

**Compound op:** `create_subclass("Drug", "...", "Substance")` (which internally
creates the class and copies N parent properties) → **one** entry
`Drug [new subclass of Substance]`, not a class-add plus N property-adds.

**Error path:** `schema.create_property("NoSuchClass", ...)` raises
`ClassNotFoundError` → no record appended; subsequent report is unaffected.

**Untracked edit:** caller does `schema.to_dict()["classes"][0]["name"] = "X"`
→ the baseline↔current diff reports the class as renamed/modified structurally,
with no semantic rename label (no op-log entry exists).

## Risks and Edge Cases
- **Rename-reconciliation / identity tracking** (central technical risk): to
  render a renamed-and-modified entity as one record and to collapse rename
  round-trips, the report must map baseline identity → current identity by
  folding rename events from the op log, then diff by identity rather than by
  current name. This is the most intricate part of the implementation and the
  area most needing thorough tests.
- **Compound/recursive operations** double-logging if the recording guard is
  wrong — must record only the outermost public call.
- **`delete_class(cascade_to_subclasses=True)`** mutates *other* classes
  (strips `subClassOf`); these appear as modifications to those classes in the
  diff — acceptable, but tests should pin the expected rendering.
- **Date-field noise** (`createdDate`/`lastModifiedDate`) must be excluded.
- **Reorder vs net effect:** a pure reorder (`assign_property_orders`) changes
  no add/remove/field — it must be detected from order comparison and/or the op
  log, or it would be invisible.
- **Legacy-format load:** `create_from` may run `SchemaTransformer.old_to_new`
  before the baseline is captured; baseline must reflect the *post-transform*
  state so the conversion itself isn't reported as changes.
- **Backward compatibility:** adding always-on tracking must not change
  `to_dict()`/`to_json()` output or any signature; existing tests must pass
  unchanged.

## Deployment Strategy
- Pure library addition; no migration, no rollout sequencing, no runtime
  dependency. Ships in the next `pydatagraphs` release.
- **Rollback:** revert the commit; no persisted state or wire-format change to
  unwind.
- **Validation:** new unit tests in `tests/schema_test.py` (net-effect
  collapse, rename+modify combination, compound ops, error paths, no
  serialisation pollution, deterministic output) plus full existing suite green.
- **Release:** `CHANGELOG.md` "Added" entry; semantic version bump (minor).

## Validated Assumptions
- "Lifetime of the instance" is **client-side, in-memory** — confirmed by
  `apply_schema` being a wholesale PUT with no server change-feed.
- Net-effect via baseline↔current diff is robust to untracked `to_dict()`
  mutations — confirmed and accepted as the desired behaviour.
- `clone()`/`create_from()` resetting the baseline is consistent with a
  construction-time baseline — confirmed.
- Plain text is acceptable as the human-readable format (markdown not required).

## Open Questions
- Exact plain-text layout/wording (grouping headers, symbols like `+`/`~`/`-`,
  count line) — cosmetic; to be finalised in the plan/implementation.
- Whether to also export anything new from `__init__.py` (likely unnecessary;
  `change_report` is a method on the already-exported `Schema`).
- Precise shape/keys of the structured record dicts — to be specified in the
  plan (a candidate shape: `{op, target, kind, change, before, after, fields}`).

## Background
The repository contains throwaway `temp/compare_schemas.py` / `analyze_schemas.py`
scripts that manually diff old vs new schema JSON — evidence that ad-hoc schema
diffing has already been a felt need. This feature replaces that ad-hoc approach
with a first-class, intent-aware capability on `Schema`. The SDK's existing
`clone()` deep-copy idiom and name-keyed `find_class`/`find_property` lookups
provide natural building blocks for baseline capture and diffing.

## Release Impact
- **bump:** `minor`
- **affected repos:** none of `engine-ts | engine-rs | parser-ts | parser-rs`
  — this work is in **`datagraphs-client-py` (`pydatagraphs`)**, which is not in
  that release set; it has its own semantic-versioning + PyPI pipeline. The
  bump level below applies to `pydatagraphs`.
- **rationale:** adds new, backward-compatible public behaviour (the
  `change_report` capability and always-on tracking) with no change to existing
  signatures or wire format → minor. Suggested branch prefix `feature/`,
  conventional-commit `feat:`.
